title: Chapter 22: Sky Necrosis
paragraphs: The ash stopped falling.

It was not a mercy of the fading nature, but a physical harbinger of catastrophe. The air above the Gates of Lamentation suddenly became dense, viscous, and icy, as if the blood had stopped in the veins of the universe itself. The sounds of Agdash—the distant clang of smithies, the moans of refugees, the whistle of the wind—all vanished at once, consumed by a deafening, crushing vacuum.

Yura, standing with a piece of chalk at the parapet, froze mid-sentence. He had just finished marking the firing sectors, which had evoked sincere, fanged respect from Janavar. But now the former office worker looked up, and the chalk slipped from his fingers.
The sky above them didn't just open. It rotted alive.

With the vile sound of a giant tendon tearing, the thinned magical barrier of the sector burst, raining down a shower of dim, dying sparks. Space turned inside out. A huge, pulsing crimson-black vortex tore the clouds, and from it poured primal darkness. It was not one of those portals that Avotu had closed before. it was a terminal necrosis of reality—a colossal breach leading into the Void itself.

"To arms!" Alexander's voice cracked, instantly raising his mag-crossbow. His earthly leader reflexes kicked in sooner than the elven signal horns on the distant towers broke out in a panic-stricken wail.

From the bleeding hole in the heavens, a landslide of Void Reapers poured out. This time they were not just brainless scavengers. Multi-jointed, grotesque creatures with asymmetrical body geometries, covered in ashy-gray armor, fell onto the stones, splashing acrid acid. Their faceted eyes glowed with a dead, hungry light. Behind them, crawling heavily on spider legs, descended colossi whose backs were bristling with pulsing bone spines.
A global breach. Agdash trembled to its very foundation.

Kirah let out a deafening, primal roar. The demon forgot his fear of elves and the crowd. In that moment, he saw only a threat to the small green being standing behind him. The titan charged forward, crashing into the first ranks of the Reapers like a black ram. His claws tore through chitinous shells with a wet crunch, and the crimson veins on his body blazed like molten magma.
Beside him, stepping heavily, Ravan joined the formation. The former dentist, a human from another dimension, shoulder to shoulder with a high demon.

"Come on then, you freak!" Ravan roared. The pneumatic cylinder on his armored arm hissed maliciously. A fierce uppercut from bottom to top slammed into the face of a pouncing Reaper. The piston fired with a deafening shot, splashing the monster's black-green blood over the stones.
"Meat and steel, Horned!" Ravan shouted to the demon, dodging an acid spit.
"I'm not horned, I'm Kirah!" the demon rasped, biting another monster in half.
"Great hook, Kirah! Hold the center!"
Janavar glided through the battlefield like a gray ghost. The werewolf moved with a frightening, bestial grace, his heavy axe leaving a trail of severed limbs behind him.
"A wolf isn't afraid of numbers," he smirked, dodging the crescent blade of a creature and hacking through its knees. "A wolf assesses the size of the buffet. Auf!"
Suddenly the air was pierced by the smell of ozone. Anar, standing on an elevation, sighed heavily, rubbing the bridge of his nose.
"How you all fuss. My eyes are swimming from you," he muttered.

The boy slowly spread his arms to the sides. His pupils vanished, flooded with pure, neon-blue light. From the heavens, straight through the clouds of ash, struck a colossal cascade of chain lightning. Discharges as thick as a tree trunk crashed down on the cluster of creatures on the left flank, incinerating them to the state of glass statues. Janavar, whose fur stood at end from the static electricity, whistled:
"The kid knows about dominance."

On the right flank, the dance of death was led by Renée. The fox had discarded her mouthpiece. In her hands shimmered two short, curved elven daggers. She moved not like a warrior, but like a ballerina, lethally and gracefully gliding between the monsters' mandibles. Each of her movements was accompanied by flashes of alchemical vials that Zhenya had carefully converted into contact grenades.

But the wave did not abate. The Void knew no mercy or fatigue. The creatures became hundreds, thousands. They began to climb the outer walls of Agdash, threatening to overwhelm the city with a living sea.
And then Avotu entered the fight.

The fire elf stood on the edge of the wall, looking at the tearing fabric of the universe. His black flame was no longer a symbol of grief. Today it burned with protection. Nature's Blade left its scabbard. The sword emitted a piercing shriek, greedily soaking up the entropy of the falling world into itself, concentrating radioactive ash and rotten mana into a dense, black whirlwind around the blade.
Avotu vanished.

He didn't run—he ceased to exist at one point and appeared in the thick of the enemies. A blurred streak of the black sword cut through space. An ideal, soul-chilling geometry of death. Shockwaves from his swings knocked down dozens of Reapers at a time, turning them into dust. The elf fought not with the rage of a berserker, but with the cold, absolute detachment of a master protecting his last home.

Sasha shot with methodical precision from his mag-crossbow at the creatures trying to come from behind Kirah and Ravan. Zhenya, smeared with soot, dragged heavy crates of pneumo-bombs assembled on his knee, tossing them into the crowd.
But there were only ten of them. Ten souls against infinity.

High in the White Hall, behind magical mirrors, the Elders of Agdash watched the Gates of Lamentation in horror. They expected this ridiculous rabble to run in the very first seconds. But they stood. A demon shielded a human with his body. An elf fought back-to-back with a werewolf. They held the breach, spitting blood and ash, breaking not only the monsters' spines but also the centuries-old prejudices of this world.
The earth shuddered from a gargantuan blow.

Out of the portal, clinging with bony hooks to the edges of the torn heavens, crawled the Void Colossus. A monster the size of a fortress tower, consisting of interwoven bodies, maws, and blades. Its roar shattered windows in the nearest quarters of the city. The creature descended heavily onto the wall of the Gates of Lamentation, crushing the marble like paper.

The strike of its colossal limb threw Kirah dozens of meters. The demon smashed through stone masonry and slumped heavily, choking on black blood. Ravan tried to strike the creature, but his armored arm was simply crumpled like a tin can, throwing the man to Sasha's feet. Anar staggered, having exhausted his mana supply—the blue sparks on his hands hissed piteously and went out.

Avotu rushed at the Colossus, but the creature opened its maw, releasing a suffocating wave of pure entropy. The elf's black flame shrank painfully, Nature's Blade grew heavy. The barrier fell. The Gates were broken.
The monster raised its thousand-ton paw over the stunned Alexander, who lay in the dust. Sasha tried to raise his crossbow, but the bolt was jammed tight by ash.
At that moment, through the chaos, rumble, and death, came the rapid patter of bare feet.
Yashil.
He had no magic. He had no pneumo-armor or ancient swords. He had only his life.
The small goblin jumped with a run-up directly under the descending limb of the Colossus, pushing the dazed Alexander aside with both hands, knocking the man out of the lethal strike zone.
The shadow of the monster covered the green-skinned figure. And time froze for everyone present.
